content: I see the issue - stripping leading/trailing whitespace removes empty fields. I need to preserve them.
reasoning_content:
The issue is that for the empty mu test, the tab-separated fields are misaligned. The first field (NLL) is empty, so the first tab is at position 0. Let me check the actual file content.

The string is: "NLL\tmu\tC\tp*\n\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2\n"
After the header, the data line is: "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2"
- Field 0 (NLL): "" (empty)
- Field 1 (mu): "" (empty)
- Field 2 (C): "2:3"
- Field 3 (p*): "0.1,0.2"

So splitting by tab should give ["", "", "2:3", "0.1,0.2"]. But the split might have issues if the first field is empty.

Actually, "".split('\t') gives [''] which is correct. But "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2".split('\t') should give ['', '', '2:3', '0.1,0.2'].

Let me check again... The error says `could not convert string to float: '2:3'` which means val='2:3' when processing NLL. So the header/data alignment is off.

Wait, the error happens because of column header mapping. The header has columns: NLL, mu, C, p*. The data has fields: '', '', '2:3', '0.1,0.2'. So:
- i=0: col_name='NLL', data[0]='' -> val='' -> NLL=None. OK.
- i=1: col_name='mu', data[1]='' -> val='' -> mu_normal=None, mu_tumors=[]. OK.
- i=2: col_name='C', data[2]='2:3' -> val='2:3' -> parse C. OK.
- i=3: col_name='p*', data[3]='0.1,0.2' -> val='0.1,0.2' -> parse p*. OK.

But the error says val='2:3' when trying float(val). That means it's selecting the wrong field.

Oh wait, I see the issue. The second test file was written after the first test and its content might have issues. Let me check the actual content of theta_test2.txt.

Actually, looking at the error trace, the first test passed (Sample result printed), but the second test failed. The file theta_test2.txt was created with content "\n\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2\n" after the header.

Wait, the string is: "NLL\tmu\tC\tp*\n\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2\n"
After splitting by '\n':
- Line 0: "NLL\tmu\tC\tp*" (header)
- Line 1: "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2" (data)
- Line 2: "" (empty line that gets skipped)

Line 1 after strip: "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2". Wait, strip() removes leading/trailing whitespace including tabs. So strip() would give "2:3\t0.1,0.2"? No, strip() only removes leading/trailing, not internal. So "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2" stripped would be "2:3\t0.1,0.2"? No, strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace characters. Leading whitespace includes the two tabs at the beginning.

So strip() on "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2" removes the leading tabs, giving "2:3\t0.1,0.2". Then split('\t') gives ["2:3", "0.1,0.2"]. So data[0]="2:3", data[1]="0.1,0.2".

And the header has 4 columns, but data only has 2 fields after stripping. So:
- col_name='NLL', data[0]='2:3' -> tries float('2:3') -> ValueError.

The issue is that strip() removed the leading tabs which represented empty fields. I should NOT strip the lines before splitting, or I should use a different approach.

Actually, the data line "\t\t2:3\t0.1,0.2" has empty first and second fields represented by leading tabs. When I strip() it, those leading tabs are removed and the fields are lost.

I should not strip the entire line before splitting. Instead, I should strip each field after splitting.

Let me fix this: don't strip the raw line, just split by tab directly.

function.name: run_custom_test
